title: Witness Giving Oath During TrialgettyWomen are not believed, one legal whitepaper finds
raw_response: {"rating": -3.1, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "Opinion/editorial arguing that the justice system systematically discounts women’s credibility and urging reforms and public awareness. The author presents a one-sided, systemic-bias framing and prescriptive solutions aligned with progressive views on structural gender inequities. This consistent advocacy without countervailing perspectives supports a Left rating of -3.1. Detailed Analysis: (1) Problem-solution framing in the lede (how and why the justice system discounts women’s credibility and what can be done) sets an advocacy tone rather than neutral inquiry. (2) Repeated assertions that women are routinely disbelieved by institutions and in court, coupled with an interpretation of a high-profile trial to illustrate that men are deemed more credible, build a single-direction narrative. (3) The piece declares that gender bias extends to family courts and treats reports and studies as confirming a broad pattern, without presenting contrary evidence or alternative interpretations (omission of balancing perspectives). (4) The author argues that judicial discretion and limited scrutiny enable harm to IPV victims and that protections must be ensured—normative institutional-change advocacy. (5) The article generalizes the issue as systemic across all phases of litigation and affecting all women, reflecting a belief in pervasive structural bias. (6) It prescribes “believing women” and recommends collaboration between legal psychologists and court actors to drive practice and policy change—support for institutional remedies. (7) It endorses social-movement frames (e.g., #MeToo) and publicizing the “credibility gap” as necessary to force confrontation of bias, aligning with progressive activism. (8) Evaluative language such as characterizing study outcomes as “startling” signals agreement with findings that reinforce the author’s thesis, with no counterweight offered."}

parsed_output.explanation: Opinion/editorial arguing that the justice system systematically discounts women’s credibility and urging reforms and public awareness. The author presents a one-sided, systemic-bias framing and prescriptive solutions aligned with progressive views on structural gender inequities. This consistent advocacy without countervailing perspectives supports a Left rating of -3.1. Detailed Analysis: (1) Problem-solution framing in the lede (how and why the justice system discounts women’s credibility and what can be done) sets an advocacy tone rather than neutral inquiry. (2) Repeated assertions that women are routinely disbelieved by institutions and in court, coupled with an interpretation of a high-profile trial to illustrate that men are deemed more credible, build a single-direction narrative. (3) The piece declares that gender bias extends to family courts and treats reports and studies as confirming a broad pattern, without presenting contrary evidence or alternative interpretations (omission of balancing perspectives). (4) The author argues that judicial discretion and limited scrutiny enable harm to IPV victims and that protections must be ensured—normative institutional-change advocacy. (5) The article generalizes the issue as systemic across all phases of litigation and affecting all women, reflecting a belief in pervasive structural bias. (6) It prescribes “believing women” and recommends collaboration between legal psychologists and court actors to drive practice and policy change—support for institutional remedies. (7) It endorses social-movement frames (e.g., #MeToo) and publicizing the “credibility gap” as necessary to force confrontation of bias, aligning with progressive activism. (8) Evaluative language such as characterizing study outcomes as “startling” signals agreement with findings that reinforce the author’s thesis, with no counterweight offered.
